title: Teaching
text:
I teach economics at School of Economics and School of Law of Université Clermont Auvergne, mainly at the undergraduate and master’s levels. My teaching covers introductory economics, economic history, macroeconomics, environmental economics, institutions, and territorial development.
A substantial part of my teaching has involved large undergraduate cohorts, including lectures in introductory economics delivered to several hundred students. I have developed online learning resources and self-assessment materials for my courses accessible on Moodle.

title: Teaching
text:
Programme responsibilities
I am Co-Director of the Master’s track in Economics and Territorial Transitions at Université Clermont Auvergne. The programme trains students to analyse, design, implement, and evaluate territorial public policies.
Its curriculum combines economic analysis with applied projects carried out in partnership with local governments and public actors. I supervise internships and collective projects involving local development, ecological transition, and public policy evaluation.
From 2013 to 2021, I was Head of the undergraduate degree in Administration and Social Sciences at the School of Law of Université Clermont Auvergne.
I also coordinate the Environment and Economic Policy module in the GPE Master’s programme, organised in partnership with FERDI and involving researchers and practitioners working on environmental and climate policy.

title: Teaching
text:
Professional training on ecological transition
Since 2024, I have served as Scientific Coordinator for a professional training programme delivered by the IHEdd and FERDI on quantitative tools for ecological transition and public policy.
The programme, Piloting the Ecological Transition: Practical Tools for Public Action, is designed for public-sector practitioners and policy professionals. It combines an online introductory module with in-person training sessions.
It provides participants with a practical understanding of three complementary families of decision-support tools:

environmental-economic accounting and natural capital accounts;
monetary valuation of environmental goods, services, and policy impacts;
integrated macro-environmental models, including GEMMES and ESTEEM.

As Scientific Coordinator, I design the overall pedagogical structure, coordinate contributions from academic and policy experts, and teach sessions on monetary valuation. The programme connects economic analysis with concrete policy issues, including climate planning, public investment, environmental budgeting, and the assessment of ecological-transition policies.

title: HDR Thesis
text: In 2022, I received my Habilitation to Supervise Research (HDR) from Université Clermont Auvergne.

title: HDR Thesis
text:
HDR examination committee
External reviewers (rapporteurs)

Tanguy Bernard, Professor, Université de Bordeaux, Bordeaux Sciences Économiques
Stéphane Blancard, Professor, AgroSup Dijon, CESAER
Raja Chakir, Research Director, INRAE, Paris-Saclay Applied Economics

Committee members

Catherine Araujo Bonjean, Research Fellow, CNRS, CERDI
Vianney Dequiedt, Professor, Université Clermont Auvergne, CERDI

My HDR thesis, La durabilité des systèmes de production agricole dans les pays en développement [Sustainability of agricultural production systems in developing countries], brings together my work on sustainable agriculture in China, land use and deforestation in Brazil, climate risks, environmental governance, and ecosystem services.
The manuscript is written in French.
Download the HDR thesis (PDF)

title: CV & Contact
text:
Short biography
I obtained a PhD in Development Economics from CERDI, Université d’Auvergne, in 2011. I subsequently held a postdoctoral position at the Federal University of Viçosa in Brazil and have been an Associate Professor at CERDI, Université Clermont Auvergne, since 2013.
My research focuses on agricultural, environmental and climate economics in developing and emerging countries, with particular attention to China, Viet Nam, Cambodia and Brazil.

title: CV & Contact

Download short CV (PDF)

title: Sébastien Marchand
text:
Development · Environmental · Agricultural Economics
I am an Associate Professor (HDR) in Economics at CERDI, Université Clermont Auvergne.
My research lies at the intersection of environmental and development economics. I study how environmental constraints, institutions, and public policies shape production decisions and economic transformation in developing countries.
I combine microeconometric methods with farm, firm, household, and country-level data. I have worked on deforestation and land-use change in the Brazilian Amazon; agricultural production, pollution, and farmer behaviour in China and Cambodia; and trade and industrial policies in forest-based sectors.
My current projects examine the effects of termite mounds on rice production and agricultural input allocation in Cambodia, the role of media sentiment in environmental regulation in China, and whether national adaptation planning affects the allocation of international climate finance.
Before joining CERDI in 2013, I was a postdoctoral researcher in Brazil, based at the Federal University of Viçosa and working in collaboration with the Institute for Applied Economic Research (IPEA) in Rio de Janeiro. I worked with José Gustavo Feres as part of the Rede Amazônia Sustentável (RAS; Sustainable Amazon Network), an interdisciplinary research initiative on the social and environmental dimensions of land-use change in the Brazilian Amazon, led by Toby Gardner.

title: Sébastien Marchand
text:
Research at a glance



Agriculture, ecosystems and productivity
How farmers and production systems respond to land, pollution, weather shocks and ecological heterogeneity in Asia and Latin America.
Explore this research →




Pollution, information and environmental governance
How industrial pollution, regulation and environmental news affect firms, households and public decision-making in China.
Explore this research →




Climate vulnerability and adaptation finance
Whether climate finance is aligned with exposure, vulnerability and national adaptation planning in developing countries.
Explore this research →




Forests, trade and structural transformation
How forest policies and trade restrictions affect employment, industrialization and resource use.
Explore this research →

title: Sébastien Marchand
text:
Selected work

Enhancing employment through log export ban: Insights from wood-processing firms in developing countries
Sébastien Marchand and Mouhamed Zerbo. Forest Policy and Economics (2025).
Using firm-level data across developing countries, this article studies how log export bans affect employment in wood-processing industries.


Mass media as an environmental watchdog? Evidence from the war on air pollution in China
Sébastien Marchand, Damien Cubizol, Elda Nasho Ah-Pine and Huanxiu Guo. Environment and Development Economics (2025).
This article examines whether the emotional tone of environmental news functions as an informal regulatory force during China’s campaign against air pollution.


The colonial origins of deforestation: an institutional analysis
Sébastien Marchand. Environment and Development Economics 21(3), 318–349 (2016).
A historical-institutional analysis of cross-country differences in deforestation across former colonies.


This starter site intentionally presents a selected research portfolio. The complete and continuously updated publication record remains available through Google Scholar, HAL and ORCID.

title: Projects
text: My research examines how environmental constraints, institutions, and public policies shape production, land use, and development in low- and middle-income countries. It combines microeconometric methods with field surveys, administrative data, environmental data, and cross-country datasets.

title: Projects
section: ECOTERM: Ecosystem services of termite mounds in Cambodia
text:
ECOTERM: Ecosystem services of termite mounds in Cambodia
ECOTERM is an interdisciplinary research project on the ecosystem services provided by termite mounds in agricultural landscapes in Cambodia. It brings together economists, ecologists, anthropologists, botanists, and soil scientists.
Within the project, I lead the economics work package. Our work investigates how termite mounds affect rice production, farmers’ input use, land allocation, and rural livelihoods. We combine repeated farm and plot-level surveys with ecological information to understand whether termite mounds act as productive assets, biodiversity reservoirs, or constraints for agricultural households.
Current work includes research on termite mounds and rice yields, as well as a study of their implications for input allocation and agricultural misallocation.
Related work

Lao, C., M. Audibert, P. Jouquet, R. Muon, K. Sok, and S. Marchand. Beyond the Surface: When Termite Mounds Affect Rice Production in Cambodia? Submitted manuscript.

title: Projects
section: Agriculture and rural environmental change in China
text:
Agriculture and rural environmental change in China
This research programme examines the sustainability of agricultural production in China, with a particular focus on family farming, input use, environmental performance, and the consequences of industrial development for rural areas.
My work has studied environmental efficiency in conventional and non-certified organic rice farming, social interactions and input spillovers among family farmers, and the effects of industrial water pollution on rice production. It shows that environmental performance depends not only on the production technology itself, but also on farmers’ experience, local interactions, and exposure to external environmental pressures.
Selected publications

Marchand, S. and H. Guo (2014). The environmental efficiency of non-certified organic farming in China: A case study of paddy rice production. China Economic Review, 31, 201–216.
Guo, H. and S. Marchand (2019). Social interactions and spillover effects in Chinese family farming. The Journal of Development Studies, 55(11), 2306–2328.
Marchand, S., M. Barro, H. Xiong, and H. Guo (2025). Industrial water pollution and farmer adaptation: Evidence from rice farming in Jiangsu, China. Agricultural Economics, 56(1), 108–123.

title: Projects
section: Media, air pollution, and environmental governance in China
text:
Media, air pollution, and environmental governance in China
This project studies the role of information and public attention in environmental regulation. It asks whether media coverage can act as an informal mechanism of accountability by influencing citizens, local governments, and firms.
Using daily data on media sentiment, air quality, weather, and industrial activity across Chinese cities, this research examines how the emotional tone of environmental news is associated with pollution outcomes. Current extensions focus on social media discussions and on firms’ emissions behaviour.
Selected publication

Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, E. Nasho Ah-Pine, and H. Guo (2025). Mass media as an environmental watchdog? Evidence from the war on air pollution in China. Environment and Development Economics, 1–25.

Current work

Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, and H. Guo. Sentiment Analysis and Air Pollution: Evidence from Weibo Posts in China.
Marchand, S., D. Cubizol, and H. Guo. Sentiment Analysis and Air Pollution: Evidence from Industrial Firms in China.

title: Projects
section: Forests, land use, and forest-based development
text:
Forests, land use, and forest-based development
My research on forests builds on my doctoral work on institutions and deforestation in developing countries, as well as on my postdoctoral experience in Brazil.
In 2012, I was a postdoctoral researcher at the Federal University of Viçosa, working in collaboration with the Institute for Applied Economic Research (IPEA) in Rio de Janeiro. I worked with José Gustavo Feres as part of the Rede Amazônia Sustentável (RAS; Sustainable Amazon Network), an interdisciplinary initiative led by Toby Gardner on the social and environmental dimensions of land-use change in the Brazilian Amazon.
This line of research has addressed the relationship between agricultural productivity and deforestation, conservation incentives for local governments, the persistence of low-income and environmentally degrading land uses, and the effects of log export bans on wood processing and employment in developing countries.
Selected publications

Marchand, S. (2012). The relationship between technical efficiency in agriculture and deforestation in the Brazilian Amazon. Ecological Economics, 77, 166–175.
Sauquet, A., S. Marchand, and J. G. Feres (2014). Protected areas, local governments, and strategic interactions: The case of the ICMS-Ecológico in the Brazilian state of Paraná. Ecological Economics, 107, 249–258.
Garrett, R. D., T. A. Gardner, T. F. Morello, S. Marchand, J. Barlow, D. Ezzine de Blas, J. Ferreira, A. C. Lees, and L. Parry (2017). Explaining the persistence of low income and environmentally degrading land uses in the Brazilian Amazon. Ecology and Society, 22(3).
Marchand, S. and M. Zerbo (2023). Revisiting the trade restrictions-industrialization nexus in developing countries: The case of log export ban and wood processing. Journal of Forest Economics, 38(2), 195–233.
Marchand, S. and M. Zerbo (2025). Enhancing employment through log export ban: Insights from wood-processing firms in developing countries. Forest Policy and Economics, 174, 103491.

title: Projects
text:
Climate risks, vulnerability, and adaptation
A more recent part of my research examines how climate risks affect production systems and how public policies can support adaptation in developing countries.
This work includes research on the effects of extreme climate events on agricultural efficiency in Vietnam, the measurement of physical vulnerability to cyclonic risks, the valuation of insurance against low-probability risks, and the allocation of international adaptation finance.
Selected publications and chapters

Mankaï, S., S. Marchand, and N. H. Le (2024). Valuing insurance against small probability risks: A meta-analysis. Journal of Behavioral and Experimental Economics, 109, 102181.
Goujon, M. and S. Marchand (2023). Measuring Vulnerability to Climate Change. In F. Cafarelli (ed.), The Resilience of Territories Exposed to Natural Hazards. Mare & Martin. [in French]
Goujon, M., S. Marchand, and O. Santoni (2023). A Comparative Measure of Territorial Vulnerability to Cyclone Activity: The Case of Vietnam. In R. Bentirou Mathlouthi and A. Pomade (eds.), Environmental Vulnerabilities: Interdisciplinary Perspectives. L’Harmattan. [in French]

Current work

Beaucoral, P., M. Goujon, and S. Marchand. No Plan, No Aid? The Effects of National Adaptation Plan Implementation on Received Adaptation Aid.

Other work

Diallo, Y., S. Marchand, and E. Espagne. Impacts of Extreme Climate Events on Technical Efficiency in Vietnamese Agriculture.

For a broader account of these research themes, see my HDR thesis.
